framing: Historian of religion evaluating independence of cross-tradition convergence
major_traditions_without_R1_R7_entity: Hard polytheism (Olympian Greek, Norse, Roman state cult): multiple competing deities, none individually satisfies R3 (universality) or R5 (inexhaustibility — gods die, are wounded, depend on ambrosia). Animism / folk Shinto: kami are local, plural, mortal — fail R3, R7, R5. Ancestor veneration (classical Confucian li, much African traditional religion): venerated ancestors fail R7 (they were once human, generated by prior input), R2 (atemporality), R5. Atheist secular humanism: explicitly denies any R1-R7 entity; treats persons-in-society as ultimate, which fails R7 and R3. Logical positivism / Carnap-Ayer: rejects meaningfulness of any R7 claim. Buddhist Theravada (strict reading): rejects atman and any substantial ultimate — though Madhyamaka emptiness functions as role, Theravada's nibbana is a state, not an entity with R1-R7. Jainism's jiva: plural souls, no single source — fails R3, R4. Manichaean dualism: two coeternal principles (light/dark) — fails uniqueness, so neither satisfies R3 individually. Zoroastrian strict dualism (pre-Zurvanite): similar — Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu both eternal. Epicurean gods: exist but irrelevant, do not source — fail R4. So roughly half of major historical traditions do NOT have an R1-R7 foundational entity. This is significant discriminating power.
what_would_strengthen_evidence: 1) Add the negative cases explicitly to CROSS_TRADITIONS.md (polytheism, animism, Jainism, Manichaeism, Epicureanism, secular humanism) showing where each fails R1-R7 — this converts the table from 'we found 14 hits' to 'we surveyed N traditions and found these pass / these fail.' 2) Pre-register an R1-R7 scoring rubric and have non-kernel-affiliated religious-studies scholars score each tradition blind to the kernel's mapping. 3) Find a tradition that has a strong R1-R7 entity but resists identification with Christian C — that would falsify the strong claim while preserving the structural one. 4) Run the reverse: derive R1-R7 from each tradition's primary text without showing the kernel, and check whether the derived properties match. The current mapping is forward-only (kernel → traditions); the reverse direction (traditions → kernel, without kernel exposure) is the cleaner test.
identification: C is the unique inhabitant of the R1-R7 role insofar as that role is coherent. The cross-tradition table is genuinely discriminating because polytheism, animism, ancestor cults, atheist humanism, and strict dualism all fail at least one R-property — it is not the case that every religion has an R1-R7 entity. Therefore the 14 traditions that DO converge are converging on something non-trivial. But the kernel is right to acknowledge (in OBJECTIONS.md 'shared-input circularity') that this is not knockdown evidence: the kernel's author selected the 14 monistic/monotheistic/process traditions because they share the structural commitment; the negative cases were not given equal billing. The convergence is real-but-curated.
honest_objection: The deepest residual concern is selection-by-inclusion-criterion: the kernel admits a tradition iff its primary entity has R1-R7, so 'all included traditions agree on R1-R7' is true by construction. The non-trivial empirical claim is the secondary one — that approximately half of major historical traditions DO have such an entity, and that the major Axial-age and post-Axial traditions cluster on the R1-R7 side rather than the polytheist / animist side. This is a real and discriminating fact, but the kernel's current presentation buries it. The cross-tradition mapping would be much stronger if it included the full survey (passes + fails) rather than only the 14 passes.
honest_assessment: The R1-R7 properties are genuinely discriminating, not loose enough to fit anything: hard polytheism, animism, ancestor veneration, strict dualism, Epicureanism, and atheist humanism all fail at least one R-property under any honest reading. So the 14 traditions that the kernel maps are not arbitrarily chosen — they share a structural commitment that roughly half of major historical traditions reject. The convergence is therefore not pure post-hoc artifact; it reflects a real distinction in how human reflective traditions have answered the pre-input question. However, the kernel's presentation is cherry-picked in that it foregrounds only the positive matches and does not display the negative cases with equal force, which is what gives the shared-input-circularity objection its grip. The honest verdict is 'partial': cross-tradition convergence is independent evidence of moderate strength for the existence of an R1-R7 role, weaker evidence for any specific identification, and the kernel itself acknowledges this in its own OBJECTIONS.md entry. The path to strengthen it is empirical and procedural: pre-registered blind scoring by non-affiliated scholars across a fuller tradition set.
